params.text:
Recognising a urinary tract infection

A urinary tract infection, or UTI, is a common problem that you can help to spot early. Because you know the people you support, you are often the first to notice that something is not right. Catching a UTI early means the person can be seen and treated before they become more unwell.

  • Urine changes: cloudy, dark or strong-smelling urine.
  • Discomfort: pain or burning when passing urine, or needing to go more often.
  • Feeling unwell: a high temperature, or new or worse confusion.

Spotting and reporting a UTI

Signs of a UTI can include needing to pass urine more often or more urgently, pain or a burning feeling when passing urine, cloudy or strong-smelling urine, lower tummy or back pain, and feeling generally unwell or feverish. In older people especially, a sudden change such as new confusion, agitation or drowsiness can be an important early sign, so never dismiss it as simply part of getting older.

Good hydration and careful hygiene help reduce the risk of infection, but if you notice any of these signs you should report them promptly to a senior colleague or nurse and record what you have seen. It is not your role to diagnose or to give any treatment — your job is to notice, report and record clearly so the person can be assessed quickly.

params.question: You notice an older person has become suddenly confused, and their urine is cloudy and strong-smelling. What should you do?

answers.text: Report it promptly to a senior colleague or nurse and record what you have observed, as these can be signs of a UTI.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. New confusion with cloudy, strong-smelling urine can signal a UTI and should be reported and recorded straight away.

answers.text: Assume the confusion is just part of getting older and do nothing.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Sudden confusion is not normal ageing — it can be an early sign of infection and must be reported.
Diagnosing and treating infection is not your role — report so the person can be assessed.

answers.text: Cut back their fluids to reduce how often they use the toilet.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Restricting fluids makes infection and dehydration more likely, not less.
